feedback.text: Like many others have said previously, I bought several games in my life. I'm an avid gamer, do it as a hobby and as a professional game developer. In my career I have indeed seen many games be filled with "traps", to keep players addicted, specific teams to study the psychological mechanisms to make these the main fundraiser for these companies ( Looking at you, mobile games ). They prey on known psychological quirks of the human mind to exploit the player to spend more and more money, or time watching ads, or hiding parts of the game, items of the game behind ads, or "eternal sales" where something is for sale with a discount until the end of days ( because seeing discounts makes the human mind feel it as a good bargain automatically, even though it sucks ). Games, as playful, joyful and recreational activities are being monetized to an extent that's abhorrent. Then, on top of that, we're now being told we don't actually buy the game with our own money, we bought a license of the game. So basically, we bought their permission to play the game while they allow us, and that they can take it away from us any time we want. That makes no sense whatsoever. It is a retrocess to 30 years ago, when you could buy a game on a CD or floppies, install it, and play it all you want, when you want. This must not continue. If I buy a car today, and in 10 years it's discontinued, and in 30 years it's a classic, I can still drive my car. It's mine. The manufacturer stopped supporting it, fine, but It's still mine, and I do with it whatever I want. If a studio decides to terminate their support for the game, so be it, but let us continue playing it. Be it with the option for players to create their own servers for multiplayer, or simple by not needing to be online for single player games. I buy a game for the recreational value. I am not willing to be a lab rat to be studied online constantly just because I'm playing their game. I don't want to be constantly online to play something that doesn't need to be online. We are the ones who pay for their products. We are the ones in charge, not them. If we don't pay, they fall. So far, the gaming community has sent a strong message. These companies will never be allowed to win with their greedy claws. If buying isn't owning, then piracy is not a crime.
